messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Inputs to generative models vary in complexity but standard
decoding allocates fixed compute per token; how can a model
adaptively allocate more or fewer computation steps without
degrading quality?


### Proposed method
We propose Adaptive-Depth Decoding (ADD), a multi-exit generative model that decides, per token, how many layers to evaluate before committing the next symbol. We attach lightweight vocabulary heads to a subset of decoder layers (e.g., every 2–4 blocks). During decoding, the model advances layer-by-layer and, at each potential exit, computes a compact set of gating features: (1) saturation via cosine similarity between the current hidden state and the previous layer’s state (and/or an EMA of deeper states), (2) predictive confidence from the current exit head (max-prob margin or entropy), and (3) cross-exit agreement (e.g., JS divergence between the last two exit distributions). A small halting module consumes these features and either exits early (skipping the remaining layers for this token) or continues deeper. A per-step decay schedule makes the halting criterion stricter for earlier tokens and more permissive later in the sequence, reflecting that early context typically benefits from more compute.

Training aligns intermediate exits to the full-depth model to avoid quality loss. First, apply deep supervision and distillation: each exit head minimizes a cross-entropy to ground truth and a KL-to-final-layer logits on teacher-forced text, ensuring shallow exits emulate the final model. Next, build a halting dataset by running the full-depth model on a corpus and retrospectively labeling, for each token and exit layer, the earliest “safe” exit that matches the final token and stays within a small log-prob KL/margin tolerance. Train the halting module to predict safe-exit decisions from its features. Finally, optionally fine-tune the halting policy with a lightweight RL phase that trades accuracy for compute: define an outcome reward based on sequence/task quality (e.g., BLEU or log-likelihood vs. full-depth) and a process reward based on calibrated confidence/consistency at the chosen exit; optimize expected reward minus a per-layer cost with a KL regularizer that keeps the policy close to the supervised halting baseline.

At inference, ADD proceeds progressively through layers for each token, evaluating the halting module after each exit head. If the predicted error risk falls below a token-position–dependent threshold, the model exits and emits the token using the current head; otherwise it evaluates more layers. To meet a target average compute budget without sacrificing quality on hard tokens, ADD adapts thresholds online with a simple PI controller: when recent average layers per token exceed the budget, it tightens thresholds slightly; when under budget, it relaxes them, while always honoring a maximum-risk cap learned during training. As an additional safeguard, a “verify-once” check triggers a one-time continuation to the next exit if the selected token’s margin is low or violates easy constraints (e.g., grammar/JSON format), preventing rare shallow mistakes from propagating.

This design yields substantial speedups because most easy tokens exit after few layers, while difficult tokens naturally consume more depth. Quality is preserved by: (i) distilling strong token distributions into intermediate exits, (ii) using saturation, confidence, and agreement together as robust evidence to stop, (iii) a conservative, decayed halting schedule early in sequences, and (iv) optional RL fine-tuning with KL control to favor compute savings only where they do not change model behavior.

### Rationale
Token difficulty varies widely across positions and prompts; fixed-depth decoding wastes compute on easy steps and starves hard ones. ADD allocates depth adaptively using signals that correlate with “no further change” if we were to evaluate more layers: cross-layer hidden-state saturation, high-confidence predictions, and cross-exit agreement. Distillation ensures shallow exits are competent proxies for the final layer, so exiting early does not degrade quality. The decayed schedule over time steps and a budget-aware threshold controller stabilize both quality and latency across sequences.

The optional RL phase turns halting into an explicit policy-optimization problem with a composite reward, letting the system discover where extra layers matter for task outcomes while a KL penalty anchors it to the supervised, high-fidelity baseline. Together, these components produce a compute-adaptive generator that preserves output quality while achieving sizable end-to-end speedups.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [MULTIMODAL] AudioGenX: Explainability on Text-to-Audio Generative Models (2502.00459)
   problem: Text-to-audio generative models lack faithful, token-level explanations of how each word in the prompt influences the generated audio, and attention-based or single-token perturbation methods miss multi-token interactions and are inefficient over long sequences.
   method:  AudioGenX learns a soft importance mask over text tokens for each generated audio token by perturbing the cross-attention mechanism and optimizing an Explainer MLP (sigmoid + Gumbel-softmax) to predict the mask. It jointly uses factual and counterfactual objectives on the latent audio-token embedding e_t: maximize cosine similarity under factual masking and minimize it under counterfactual masking, with L1/L2 regularization for sparse, simple masks. Explanations are computed per audio token (parallelizable) and then aggregated over time to provide holistic or interval-specific explanations. The approach supports simultaneous multi-token perturbations and is model-agnostic for TAG models that use cross-attention.

2. [LLM_NLP] Exploring Design Choices for Building Language-Specific LLMs (2406.14670)
   problem: Initializing embeddings for newly added tokens can make or break adaptation quality, but many proposed methods require external resources or extra training.
   method:  Evaluate five strategies for new‑token initialization: random, random existing token, mean of constituent subtokens, FOCUS (semantic similarity in an auxiliary static embedding space), and a Learned‑Emb warm‑start. The simple mean‑of‑constituents initialization consistently matches or outperforms more complex alternatives across tasks and languages (e.g., on Hindi and Tamil), providing a robust, resource‑light default; a brief embedding‑only warm‑start speeds convergence but yields modest final gains.

3. [MULTIMODAL] You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model (2211.11152)
   problem: Unified vision-language (encoder-decoder) models incur high inference cost and existing early-exit methods either target encoder-only models or only the decoder, since estimating encoder-side confidence is hard; this leads to suboptimal compute savings.
   method:  MuE introduces dynamic early exiting in both encoder and decoder by using layer-wise input similarity as the exit signal. It measures cosine similarity of hidden states across layers to detect saturation; when similarity exceeds a threshold, subsequent layers are skipped. This multiple-exit mechanism applies per decoding step (with a decayed threshold for autoregressive generation) and achieves large speedups (e.g., up to ~50% expected time reduction on SNLI-VE and ~40% on COCO) with minimal performance drop.

4. [MULTIMODAL] End-to-End Transformer Based Model for Image Captioning (2203.15350)
   problem: In Transformer-based captioning, vision–language interaction typically occurs only at cross-attention, leaving the decoder without global visual context early in processing, which weakens reasoning over the image.
   method:  PureT introduces a pre-fusion module in every decoder block that concatenates the refined global visual feature with previously generated word embeddings before masked self-attention, providing an early inter-modal interaction. This is followed by language masked self-attention and then cross-attention over the refined grid features. Ablation studies show the pre-fusion consistently improves all metrics compared to variants without it.

5. [MULTIMODAL] Plug-and-Play VQA: Zero-shot VQA by Conjoining Large Pretrained Models with Zero Training (2210.08773)
   problem: Zero-shot VQA needs to reason over images and questions without any additional multimodal training; generic, image-wide captions often miss question-relevant details, limiting a text-only QA model’s effectiveness.
   method:  Use a pretrained VLM (BLIP) both to localize question-relevant regions and to generate diverse, question-guided captions. Relevance is computed by a GradCAM-style aggregation over BLIP’s cross-attention in the Image-grounded Text Encoder: multiply cross-attention scores by gradients of the image–text similarity and average over heads/tokens to get per-patch relevance, then sample K′ patches per caption proportionally to this relevance. From these patches, generate N diverse captions via stochastic top-k decoding (e.g., k=50) and drop near-duplicates. This produces captions with higher answer-hit rates and boosts zero-shot VQA accuracy, outperforming generic/random patch captioning and even 5 human captions when using 100 question-guided captions.

   problem: End-to-end neural VQA systems are opaque and struggle on questions that require extra background knowledge and explicit multi-step reasoning, especially for what/which questions over unconstrained images.
   method:  Add an explicit probabilistic reasoning layer atop neural VQA by formulating VQA as PSL inference over semantic graphs from both the image and the question. Image semantics are obtained by parsing Dense Captioning outputs into triplets has_img(X, R, Y); question semantics into has_q(X, R, Y). The PSL program is augmented with phrasal similarity (≈p) derived from word2vec and optionally ConceptNet 5.5 to allow fuzzy matching between open-ended phrases, models a potential has_img_ans(Z, X, R, Y) linking answers to image triplets, includes rules that soft-match question and image structures (including missing-node matches), and imposes a summation constraint over ans(Z) to prioritize the most supported answers. Rule weights are learned by maximum likelihood. The system outputs both answers and key evidential predicates, and improves accuracy particularly on specific what/which categories in VQA.

   method:  Insert a heavily-parameterized token-level MLP prediction head atop the ViT trunk that processes each patch independently before projecting to pixels. This head captures low-level signals needed for accurate pixel regression, keeping the trunk focused on higher-level, transferable representations. Experiments show the MLP head significantly improves downstream performance over having no head, a transformer head brings only marginal gains at much higher cost, and increasing head depth (more MLP blocks) is especially beneficial.

8. [MULTIMODAL] Text-image Alignment for Diffusion-based Perception (2310.00031)
   problem: Diffusion-pretrained backbones have strong generative priors, but diffusion-based perception lacks a principled prompting strategy; unaligned prompts (e.g., averaged EOS embeddings) misalign text and image features via cross-attention, hurting tasks like semantic segmentation and depth estimation.
   method:  Text-Aligned Diffusion Perception (TADP) conditions Stable Diffusion with automated per-image captions generated by BLIP-2, aligning text to each input image. It runs a single reverse-diffusion step ϵθ(z0, 0, C), concatenates cross-attention maps and multi-scale U-Net features, and feeds them to a task-specific head, with latent scaling applied to image latents. Compared to prior averaging of EOS tokens, this per-image caption conditioning yields sharper, targeted cross-attention and improves performance across benchmarks (e.g., VOC mIoU increases by ~4 over VPD(LS), ADE20K improves, and NYUv2 RMSE drops from 0.235 to 0.225). Ablations show longer captions help and that nouns are largely sufficient, indicating the mechanism benefits from precise, image-aligned semantics.

   problem: Designing a general-purpose vision backbone that balances accuracy and efficiency across classification, detection, and segmentation while modeling long-range dependencies without deep stacking.
   method:  ScalableViT is a hierarchical ViT backbone that alternates IW-MSA and SSA blocks within each stage and inserts a positional encoding generator (PEG) at the front of stages. This topology leverages SSA’s context-preserving scaling and IWSA’s inter-window interaction to capture long-range dependencies in single layers, improving the accuracy–cost trade-off. Empirically, ScalableViT-S improves ImageNet-1K top-1 by +1.4% over Twins-SVT-S and +1.8% over Swin-T at similar FLOPs, and yields substantial gains on COCO detection and ADE20K segmentation.

10. [RL] Speeding up Policy Simulation in Supply Chain RL (2406.01939)
   problem: In supply chain RL (and many long-horizon MDPs), simulating a single trajectory under a state-dependent policy is a bottleneck because policy evaluations are inherently serial and expensive (often DNN-based), while standard parallel discrete event methods like Time Warp suffer rollbacks and GPU-unfriendly message passing.
   method:  Introduce Picard iteration for policy simulation: split the horizon [T] across M (virtual) processes and initialize a cache of actions for all time-steps. In each iteration, every process sweeps the entire trajectory but evaluates the policy π only on its assigned time-steps, using cached actions elsewhere (or aϕ if infeasible), while applying the cheap system transition f at every step; at iteration end, each process writes its evaluated actions back to the cache. This enables batched, GPU-friendly evaluation of π with no message passing and provably converges to the sequential trajectory in at most T iterations (Algorithm 1; correctness Proposition 2.1; batching and speedup discussion).

This estimator guides offline model selection and aligns with observed online outcomes, filtering policies that would degrade user experience.

At deployment, the policy performs K-rollout inner-loop updates with vanilla policy gradient to quickly adapt to the current dynamics, while the outer meta-update backpropagates through the inner steps and is optimized with PPO. Develop an AllPairs estimator that harvests implicit interventions from logs of at least two historic rankers (e.g., A/B tests or stationary deployments): define interventional sets S_{k,k'} of (x, d) that appear at both positions under different rankers, compute assignment weights q_k(x, d), form IPS-weighted click labels, and jointly fit h and an average-relevance model g(k, k', x) by maximizing a weighted cross-entropy objective. At search time, it selects all tokens for short queries and only the top-K_T2 tokens by average score for long queries, avoiding neural computation on queries. This keeps query dispatch overhead negligible while preserving the lexical benefits that improve candidate recall and overall efficiency.

A format-correctness reward and a trajectory-dependent scaling factor λ_T upweight process rewards when the final answer is correct and downweight them otherwise (§3.3.1). Training with PPO and a KL penalty (§3.3.2) yields measurable gains; ablations show removing the process reward drops average accuracy by ~1.4 points and removing all RL rewards by an additional ~3.6 points (Table 2).

   method:  IMP-HRL augments a hierarchical RL (MAPLE-style) framework with an impedance primitive: the low-level parameter policy outputs both target positions and per-axis stiffness parameters (Kx, Ky, Kz) plus rotational stiffness Kψ, while damping is set via critical damping to reduce learnable parameters.    problem: Tight-tolerance robotic assembly requires precise, contact-aware control, but trajectory-centric RL with direct torque actions struggles to leverage contact constraints and remain stable in free space.
   method:  Make the policy’s action an operational-space wrench F_tip and execute it with a hybrid motion/force controller: project desired wrenches to joint torques via J^T pseudoinverse, and wrap with a low-gain position loop with motion/force weightings (Σ1, Σ2) for stability. Ablations show self-attention fusion is crucial and pretrained audio features transfer well—even when frozen—highlighting the effectiveness of the fusion design and pretraining choices.

1. FFN-SkipLLM: A Hidden Gem for Autoregressive Decoding with Adaptive Feed Forward Skipping (Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2024, cites: 26)
   Autoregressive Large Language Models (e.g., LLaMa, GPTs) are omnipresent achieving remarkable success in language understanding and generation. However, such impressive capability typically comes with a substantial model size, which presents significant challenges for autoregressive token-by-token generation. To mitigate computation overload incurred during generation, several early-exit and layer-dropping strategies have been proposed. Despite some promising success due to the redundancy across LLMs layers on metrics like Rough-L/BLUE, our careful knowledge-intensive evaluation unveils issues such as generation collapse, hallucination, and noticeable performance drop even at the trivial exit ratio of ~10-15% of layers. We attribute these errors primarily to ineffective handling of the KV cache through state copying during early exit. In this work, we observe the saturation of computationally expensive feed-forward blocks of LLM layers and propose FFN-SkipLLM, which is a novel fine-grained skip strategy for autoregressive LLMs. FFN-SkipLLM leverages an input-adaptive feed-forward skipping approach that can skip ~25-30% of FFN blocks of LLMs with marginal change in performance on knowledge-intensive generation tasks without any requirement to handle the KV cache. Our extensive experiments and ablation studies across benchmarks like MT-Bench, Factoid-QA, and variable-length text summarization illustrate how our simple and easy-to-use method can facilitate faster autoregressive decoding.

2. Fast and Robust Early-Exiting Framework for Autoregressive Language Models with Synchronized Parallel Decoding (Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2023, cites: 92)
   To tackle the high inference latency exhibited by autoregressive language models, previous studies have proposed an early-exiting framework that allocates adaptive computation paths for each token based on the complexity of generating the subsequent token. However, we observed several shortcomings, including performance degradation caused by a state copying mechanism or numerous exit paths, and sensitivity to exit confidence thresholds. Consequently, we propose a Fast and Robust Early-Exiting (FREE) framework, which incorporates a shallow-deep module and a synchronized parallel decoding. Our framework enables faster inference by synchronizing the decoding process of the current token with previously stacked early-exited tokens. Furthermore, as parallel decoding allows us to observe predictions from both shallow and deep models, we present a novel adaptive threshold estimator that exploits a Beta mixture model to determine suitable confidence thresholds. We empirically demonstrated the superiority of our proposed framework on extensive generation tasks.

3. Saber: An Efficient Sampling with Adaptive Acceleration and Backtracking Enhanced Remasking for Diffusion Language Model (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 9)
   Diffusion language models (DLMs) are emerging as a compelling alternative to the dominant autoregressive paradigm, offering inherent advantages in parallel generation and bidirectional context modeling. However, for the tasks with strict structural constraints such as code generation, DLMs face a critical trade-off between inference speed and output quality, where accelerating generation by reducing sampling steps often leads to catastrophic performance collapse. We find that the fundamental reasons are: 1) the generation difficulty is non-uniform in the structured sequence decoding steps, making DLM's static acceleration strategy suboptimal; 2) the context of tokens generated by DLM evolves continuously, causing early high-confidence predictions to turn into irreversible errors. In this paper, we introduce efficient Sampling with Adaptive acceleration and Backtracking Enhanced Remasking (i.e., Saber), a novel training-free sampling algorithm for DLMs that first achieves both better inference speed and output quality in code generation. Saber dynamically adjusts the number of tokens unmasked per step based on the model's evolving confidence, and utilizes a backtracking mechanism to revert tokens whose confidence drops as new context emerges, with its effectiveness supported by theoretical analysis. Extensive experiments on multiple mainstream code generation benchmarks show that Saber boosts Pass@1 accuracy by an average of 1.9\% over mainstream DLM sampling methods, while achieving an average 251.4\% inference speedup. By leveraging the inherent advantages of DLMs, our work significantly narrows the performance gap with autoregressive models in code generation.

   Transformer-based language models have recently gained popularity in numerous natural language processing (NLP) applications due to their superior performance compared to traditional algorithms. These models involve two execution stages: summarization and generation. The generation stage accounts for a significant portion of the total execution time due to its auto-regressive property, which necessitates considerable and repetitive off-chip accesses. Consequently, our objective is to minimize off-chip accesses during the generation stage to expedite transformer execution. To achieve the goal, we propose a token-adaptive early exit (ToEx) that generates output tokens using fewer decoders, thereby reducing off-chip accesses for loading weight parameters. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a methodology that facilitates accurate self-attention by lazily performing computations for previously exited tokens. 5. TIDE: Token-Informed Depth Execution for Per-Token Early Exit in LLM Inference ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Large language models run every token through every layer, regardless of difficulty. We present TIDE, a post-training system that attaches tiny learned routers at periodic checkpoint layers and, at inference time, selects the earliest layer whose hidden state has converged for each token. TIDE requires no model retraining, works with any HuggingFace causal LM, auto-detects GPU architecture, and supports float32, float16, and bfloat16 through fused CUDA kernels. On an NVIDIA A100 with DeepSeek R1 Distill 8B, TIDE achieves 100% prefill exit rate (5% of tokens exit at layer 11, the remaining at layer 31), reduces prefill latency by 7.2%, and increases single-batch throughput by 6.6%. During autoregressive decoding, 98-99% of tokens exit early while the model correctly solves a multi-step math problem with 95 unique output tokens. On Qwen3 8B (36 layers), throughput improves by 8.1% at batch size 8. Calibration on 2,000 WikiText samples takes under 3 minutes and produces a ~4 MB router checkpoint. The system comprises 1,308 lines of Python and 1,081 lines of CUDA/C++ with 74 passing tests. Code: https://github.com/RightNow-AI/TIDE

6. Accelerating Large Language Model Inference via Early-Exiting Algorithms (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Large language models have achieved remarkable capabilities, but their practical deployment is hindered by significant computational costs. While adaptive computation methods like early-exiting promise to reduce these costs, they introduce a fundamental conflict: the per-token dynamism intended to save computation often creates system-level bottlenecks that can paradoxically reduce throughput in batched inference. This dissertation resolves this conflict by co-designing adaptive algorithms and model architectures to strike an optimal balance between dynamism and efficiency. To this end, our work first addresses critical sources of overhead in conventional early-exiting by proposing an efficient parallel decoding mechanism. We then show that deep parameter sharing provides an architectural foundation that not only yields compact, parameter-efficient models but also inherently mitigates the critical synchronization issues affecting dynamic inference. Finally, this work presents a unified framework where lightweight routers are pretrained to dynamically assign an optimal recursion depth for each token. This approach establishes a new Pareto frontier between efficiency and performance by effectively optimizing for both adaptive computation and parameter efficiency within a single model.

7. DEL: Context-Aware Dynamic Exit Layer for Efficient Self-Speculative Decoding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)
   Speculative Decoding (SD) is a widely used approach to accelerate the inference of large language models (LLMs) without reducing generation quality. It operates by first using a compact model to draft multiple tokens efficiently, followed by parallel verification using the target LLM. This approach leads to faster inference compared to auto-regressive decoding. While there are multiple approaches to create a draft model, one promising approach is to use early-exit methods. These methods draft candidate tokens by using a subset of layers of the primary model and applying the remaining layers for verification, allowing a single model to handle both drafting and verification. While this technique reduces memory usage and computational cost, its performance relies on the choice of the exit layer for drafting and the number of tokens drafted (speculation length) in each SD round. Prior works use hyperparameter exploration to statically select these values. However, our evaluations show that these hyperparameter values are task-specific, and even within a task they are dependent on the current sequence context. We introduce DEL (Dynamic Exit Layer), a plug-and-play method that adaptively selects the exit layer and speculation length during inference. DEL dynamically tracks the token acceptance rate if the tokens are drafted at each layer of an LLM and uses that knowledge to heuristically select the optimal exit layer and speculation length. Our experiments across a broad range of models and downstream tasks show that DEL achieves overall speedups of $2.16\times$$\sim$$2.62\times$ over vanilla auto-regressive decoding and improves upon state-of-the-art SD methods, which peak at $2.43\times$, by up to $0.19\times$. The code is available at https://github.com/hoenza/DEL.

The full latency–quality Pareto frontier is characterized across three translation benchmarks—WMT16 English–Romanian (En–Ro), WMT16 English–German (En–De), and WMT14 English–French (En–Fr)—spanning four operating points (<inline-formula> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda _{2} \in \{0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40\}$ </tex-math></inline-formula>). At the optimal operating point (2=0.05), the proposed model achieves 96% baseline translation quality (BLEU scores for En-Ro, 61.4 and 63.9) at a <inline-formula> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$1.21\times $ </tex-math></inline-formula> increase in processing speed and a 95 th -percentile latency decrease from 51.4 ms / token to 42.6 ms / token. 9. Judge Decoding: Faster Speculative Sampling Requires Going Beyond Model Alignment (International Conference on Learning Representations 2025, cites: 47)
   The performance of large language models (LLMs) is closely linked to their underlying size, leading to ever-growing networks and hence slower inference. Speculative decoding has been proposed as a technique to accelerate autoregressive generation, leveraging a fast draft model to propose candidate tokens, which are then verified in parallel based on their likelihood under the target model. While this approach guarantees to reproduce the target output, it incurs a substantial penalty: many high-quality draft tokens are rejected, even when they represent objectively valid continuations. Indeed, we show that even powerful draft models such as GPT-4o, as well as human text cannot achieve high acceptance rates under the standard verification scheme. This severely limits the speedup potential of current speculative decoding methods, as an early rejection becomes overwhelmingly likely when solely relying on alignment of draft and target. We thus ask the following question: Can we adapt verification to recognize correct, but non-aligned replies? To this end, we draw inspiration from the LLM-as-a-judge framework, which demonstrated that LLMs are able to rate answers in a versatile way. We carefully design a dataset to elicit the same capability in the target model by training a compact module on top of the embeddings to produce ``judgements"of the current continuation. We showcase our strategy on the Llama-3.1 family, where our 8b/405B-Judge achieves a speedup of 9x over Llama-405B, while maintaining its quality on a large range of benchmarks. These benefits remain present even in optimized inference frameworks, where our method reaches up to 141 tokens/s for 8B/70B-Judge and 129 tokens/s for 8B/405B on 2 and 8 H100s respectively.

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Inputs to generative models vary in complexity but standard
decoding allocates fixed compute per token; how can a model
adaptively allocate more or fewer computation steps without
degrading quality?


### Proposed method
We propose Adaptive-Depth Decoding (ADD), a multi-exit generative model that decides, per token, how many layers to evaluate before committing the next symbol. We attach lightweight vocabulary heads to a subset of decoder layers (e.g., every 2–4 blocks). During decoding, the model advances layer-by-layer and, at each potential exit, computes a compact set of gating features: (1) saturation via cosine similarity between the current hidden state and the previous layer’s state (and/or an EMA of deeper states), (2) predictive confidence from the current exit head (max-prob margin or entropy), and (3) cross-exit agreement (e.g., JS divergence between the last two exit distributions). A small halting module consumes these features and either exits early (skipping the remaining layers for this token) or continues deeper. A per-step decay schedule makes the halting criterion stricter for earlier tokens and more permissive later in the sequence, reflecting that early context typically benefits from more compute.

Training aligns intermediate exits to the full-depth model to avoid quality loss. First, apply deep supervision and distillation: each exit head minimizes a cross-entropy to ground truth and a KL-to-final-layer logits on teacher-forced text, ensuring shallow exits emulate the final model. Next, build a halting dataset by running the full-depth model on a corpus and retrospectively labeling, for each token and exit layer, the earliest “safe” exit that matches the final token and stays within a small log-prob KL/margin tolerance. Train the halting module to predict safe-exit decisions from its features. Finally, optionally fine-tune the halting policy with a lightweight RL phase that trades accuracy for compute: define an outcome reward based on sequence/task quality (e.g., BLEU or log-likelihood vs. full-depth) and a process reward based on calibrated confidence/consistency at the chosen exit; optimize expected reward minus a per-layer cost with a KL regularizer that keeps the policy close to the supervised halting baseline.

At inference, ADD proceeds progressively through layers for each token, evaluating the halting module after each exit head. If the predicted error risk falls below a token-position–dependent threshold, the model exits and emits the token using the current head; otherwise it evaluates more layers. To meet a target average compute budget without sacrificing quality on hard tokens, ADD adapts thresholds online with a simple PI controller: when recent average layers per token exceed the budget, it tightens thresholds slightly; when under budget, it relaxes them, while always honoring a maximum-risk cap learned during training. As an additional safeguard, a “verify-once” check triggers a one-time continuation to the next exit if the selected token’s margin is low or violates easy constraints (e.g., grammar/JSON format), preventing rare shallow mistakes from propagating.

This design yields substantial speedups because most easy tokens exit after few layers, while difficult tokens naturally consume more depth. Quality is preserved by: (i) distilling strong token distributions into intermediate exits, (ii) using saturation, confidence, and agreement together as robust evidence to stop, (iii) a conservative, decayed halting schedule early in sequences, and (iv) optional RL fine-tuning with KL control to favor compute savings only where they do not change model behavior.

### Rationale
Token difficulty varies widely across positions and prompts; fixed-depth decoding wastes compute on easy steps and starves hard ones. ADD allocates depth adaptively using signals that correlate with “no further change” if we were to evaluate more layers: cross-layer hidden-state saturation, high-confidence predictions, and cross-exit agreement. Distillation ensures shallow exits are competent proxies for the final layer, so exiting early does not degrade quality. The decayed schedule over time steps and a budget-aware threshold controller stabilize both quality and latency across sequences.

The optional RL phase turns halting into an explicit policy-optimization problem with a composite reward, letting the system discover where extra layers matter for task outcomes while a KL penalty anchors it to the supervised, high-fidelity baseline. Together, these components produce a compute-adaptive generator that preserves output quality while achieving sizable end-to-end speedups.

1. FFN-SkipLLM: A Hidden Gem for Autoregressive Decoding with Adaptive Feed Forward Skipping (Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2024, cites: 26)
   Autoregressive Large Language Models (e.g., LLaMa, GPTs) are omnipresent achieving remarkable success in language understanding and generation. However, such impressive capability typically comes with a substantial model size, which presents significant challenges for autoregressive token-by-token generation. To mitigate computation overload incurred during generation, several early-exit and layer-dropping strategies have been proposed. Despite some promising success due to the redundancy across LLMs layers on metrics like Rough-L/BLUE, our careful knowledge-intensive evaluation unveils issues such as generation collapse, hallucination, and noticeable performance drop even at the trivial exit ratio of ~10-15% of layers. We attribute these errors primarily to ineffective handling of the KV cache through state copying during early exit. In this work, we observe the saturation of computationally expensive feed-forward blocks of LLM layers and propose FFN-SkipLLM, which is a novel fine-grained skip strategy for autoregressive LLMs. FFN-SkipLLM leverages an input-adaptive feed-forward skipping approach that can skip ~25-30% of FFN blocks of LLMs with marginal change in performance on knowledge-intensive generation tasks without any requirement to handle the KV cache. Our extensive experiments and ablation studies across benchmarks like MT-Bench, Factoid-QA, and variable-length text summarization illustrate how our simple and easy-to-use method can facilitate faster autoregressive decoding.

2. Fast and Robust Early-Exiting Framework for Autoregressive Language Models with Synchronized Parallel Decoding (Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing 2023, cites: 92)
   To tackle the high inference latency exhibited by autoregressive language models, previous studies have proposed an early-exiting framework that allocates adaptive computation paths for each token based on the complexity of generating the subsequent token. However, we observed several shortcomings, including performance degradation caused by a state copying mechanism or numerous exit paths, and sensitivity to exit confidence thresholds. Consequently, we propose a Fast and Robust Early-Exiting (FREE) framework, which incorporates a shallow-deep module and a synchronized parallel decoding. Our framework enables faster inference by synchronizing the decoding process of the current token with previously stacked early-exited tokens. Furthermore, as parallel decoding allows us to observe predictions from both shallow and deep models, we present a novel adaptive threshold estimator that exploits a Beta mixture model to determine suitable confidence thresholds. We empirically demonstrated the superiority of our proposed framework on extensive generation tasks.

3. Saber: An Efficient Sampling with Adaptive Acceleration and Backtracking Enhanced Remasking for Diffusion Language Model (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 9)
   Diffusion language models (DLMs) are emerging as a compelling alternative to the dominant autoregressive paradigm, offering inherent advantages in parallel generation and bidirectional context modeling. However, for the tasks with strict structural constraints such as code generation, DLMs face a critical trade-off between inference speed and output quality, where accelerating generation by reducing sampling steps often leads to catastrophic performance collapse. We find that the fundamental reasons are: 1) the generation difficulty is non-uniform in the structured sequence decoding steps, making DLM's static acceleration strategy suboptimal; 2) the context of tokens generated by DLM evolves continuously, causing early high-confidence predictions to turn into irreversible errors. In this paper, we introduce efficient Sampling with Adaptive acceleration and Backtracking Enhanced Remasking (i.e., Saber), a novel training-free sampling algorithm for DLMs that first achieves both better inference speed and output quality in code generation. Saber dynamically adjusts the number of tokens unmasked per step based on the model's evolving confidence, and utilizes a backtracking mechanism to revert tokens whose confidence drops as new context emerges, with its effectiveness supported by theoretical analysis. Extensive experiments on multiple mainstream code generation benchmarks show that Saber boosts Pass@1 accuracy by an average of 1.9\% over mainstream DLM sampling methods, while achieving an average 251.4\% inference speedup. By leveraging the inherent advantages of DLMs, our work significantly narrows the performance gap with autoregressive models in code generation.

   Transformer-based language models have recently gained popularity in numerous natural language processing (NLP) applications due to their superior performance compared to traditional algorithms. These models involve two execution stages: summarization and generation. To overcome these challenges, we introduce a methodology that facilitates accurate self-attention by lazily performing computations for previously exited tokens. 5. TIDE: Token-Informed Depth Execution for Per-Token Early Exit in LLM Inference ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Large language models run every token through every layer, regardless of difficulty. We present TIDE, a post-training system that attaches tiny learned routers at periodic checkpoint layers and, at inference time, selects the earliest layer whose hidden state has converged for each token. TIDE requires no model retraining, works with any HuggingFace causal LM, auto-detects GPU architecture, and supports float32, float16, and bfloat16 through fused CUDA kernels. On an NVIDIA A100 with DeepSeek R1 Distill 8B, TIDE achieves 100% prefill exit rate (5% of tokens exit at layer 11, the remaining at layer 31), reduces prefill latency by 7.2%, and increases single-batch throughput by 6.6%. During autoregressive decoding, 98-99% of tokens exit early while the model correctly solves a multi-step math problem with 95 unique output tokens. On Qwen3 8B (36 layers), throughput improves by 8.1% at batch size 8. Calibration on 2,000 WikiText samples takes under 3 minutes and produces a ~4 MB router checkpoint. 6. Accelerating Large Language Model Inference via Early-Exiting Algorithms (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Large language models have achieved remarkable capabilities, but their practical deployment is hindered by significant computational costs. While adaptive computation methods like early-exiting promise to reduce these costs, they introduce a fundamental conflict: the per-token dynamism intended to save computation often creates system-level bottlenecks that can paradoxically reduce throughput in batched inference. This dissertation resolves this conflict by co-designing adaptive algorithms and model architectures to strike an optimal balance between dynamism and efficiency. To this end, our work first addresses critical sources of overhead in conventional early-exiting by proposing an efficient parallel decoding mechanism. We then show that deep parameter sharing provides an architectural foundation that not only yields compact, parameter-efficient models but also inherently mitigates the critical synchronization issues affecting dynamic inference. Finally, this work presents a unified framework where lightweight routers are pretrained to dynamically assign an optimal recursion depth for each token. This approach establishes a new Pareto frontier between efficiency and performance by effectively optimizing for both adaptive computation and parameter efficiency within a single model.

7. DEL: Context-Aware Dynamic Exit Layer for Efficient Self-Speculative Decoding (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 5)
   Speculative Decoding (SD) is a widely used approach to accelerate the inference of large language models (LLMs) without reducing generation quality. It operates by first using a compact model to draft multiple tokens efficiently, followed by parallel verification using the target LLM. This approach leads to faster inference compared to auto-regressive decoding. While there are multiple approaches to create a draft model, one promising approach is to use early-exit methods. These methods draft candidate tokens by using a subset of layers of the primary model and applying the remaining layers for verification, allowing a single model to handle both drafting and verification. While this technique reduces memory usage and computational cost, its performance relies on the choice of the exit layer for drafting and the number of tokens drafted (speculation length) in each SD round. Prior works use hyperparameter exploration to statically select these values. However, our evaluations show that these hyperparameter values are task-specific, and even within a task they are dependent on the current sequence context. We introduce DEL (Dynamic Exit Layer), a plug-and-play method that adaptively selects the exit layer and speculation length during inference. DEL dynamically tracks the token acceptance rate if the tokens are drafted at each layer of an LLM and uses that knowledge to heuristically select the optimal exit layer and speculation length. Our experiments across a broad range of models and downstream tasks show that DEL achieves overall speedups of $2.16\times$$\sim$$2.62\times$ over vanilla auto-regressive decoding and improves upon state-of-the-art SD methods, which peak at $2.43\times$, by up to $0.19\times$. The code is available at https://github.com/hoenza/DEL.

These results provide insight into the latency-quality trade-off and show adaptive, confidence-guided depth-allocation as an efficient layer to improve performance for latency-sensitive MT applications.

9. Judge Decoding: Faster Speculative Sampling Requires Going Beyond Model Alignment (International Conference on Learning Representations 2025, cites: 47)
   The performance of large language models (LLMs) is closely linked to their underlying size, leading to ever-growing networks and hence slower inference. Speculative decoding has been proposed as a technique to accelerate autoregressive generation, leveraging a fast draft model to propose candidate tokens, which are then verified in parallel based on their likelihood under the target model. While this approach guarantees to reproduce the target output, it incurs a substantial penalty: many high-quality draft tokens are rejected, even when they represent objectively valid continuations. Indeed, we show that even powerful draft models such as GPT-4o, as well as human text cannot achieve high acceptance rates under the standard verification scheme. This severely limits the speedup potential of current speculative decoding methods, as an early rejection becomes overwhelmingly likely when solely relying on alignment of draft and target. We thus ask the following question: Can we adapt verification to recognize correct, but non-aligned replies? To this end, we draw inspiration from the LLM-as-a-judge framework, which demonstrated that LLMs are able to rate answers in a versatile way. We carefully design a dataset to elicit the same capability in the target model by training a compact module on top of the embeddings to produce ``judgements"of the current continuation. We showcase our strategy on the Llama-3.1 family, where our 8b/405B-Judge achieves a speedup of 9x over Llama-405B, while maintaining its quality on a large range of benchmarks. These benefits remain present even in optimized inference frameworks, where our method reaches up to 141 tokens/s for 8B/70B-Judge and 129 tokens/s for 8B/405B on 2 and 8 H100s respectively.

10. Hierarchical Skip Decoding for Efficient Autoregressive Text Generation (arXiv.org 2024, cites: 6)
   Autoregressive decoding strategy is a commonly used method for text generation tasks with pre-trained language models, while early-exiting is an effective approach to speedup the inference stage. In this work, we propose a novel decoding strategy named Hierarchical Skip Decoding (HSD) for efficient autoregressive text generation. Different from existing methods that require additional trainable components, HSD is a plug-and-play method applicable to autoregressive text generation models, it adaptively skips decoding layers in a hierarchical manner based on the current sequence length, thereby reducing computational workload and allocating computation resources. Comprehensive experiments on five text generation datasets with pre-trained language models demonstrate HSD's advantages in balancing efficiency and text quality. With almost half of the layers skipped, HSD can sustain 90% of the text quality compared to vanilla autoregressive decoding, outperforming the competitive approaches.

We introduce a multi-objective hardware-in-the-loop framework that optimizes accuracy, latency, energy, and memory jointly through: (i) hardware-specific cost-model-guided adaptive multi-granularity pruning, (ii) hierarchical knowledge distillation with latency-energy regularization, and (iii) energy-cognizant dynamic early-exit mechanisms with reinforcement learning-driven optimization. Unlike traditional methods that rely solely on statistical features and blind trial-and-error, LLaPipe leverages the semantic understanding capabilities of LLMs to provide contextually relevant exploration guidance. Our framework introduces three key innovations: (1) an LLM Policy Advisor that analyzes dataset semantics and pipeline history to suggest promising preprocessing operations, (2) an Experience Distillation mechanism that mines successful patterns from past pipelines and transfers this knowledge to guide future exploration, and (3) an Adaptive Advisor Triggering strategy (Advisor\textsuperscript{+}) that dynamically determines when LLM intervention is most beneficial, balancing exploration effectiveness with computational cost. Through extensive experiments on 18 diverse datasets spanning multiple domains, we demonstrate that LLaPipe achieves up to 22.4\% improvement in pipeline quality and 2.3$\times$ faster convergence compared to state-of-the-art RL-based methods, while maintaining computational efficiency through selective LLM usage (averaging only 19.0\% of total exploration steps).

Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models leverage large-scale multimodal pretraining to provide generalist, task-level reasoning, but current limitations hinder their direct use in fast and precise manipulation. In this paper, we propose Vision-Language-Action Jump-Starting (VLAJS), a method that bridges sparse VLA guidance with on-policy RL to improve exploration and learning efficiency. VLAJS treats VLAs as transient sources of high-level action suggestions that bias early exploration and improve credit assignment, while preserving the high-frequency, state-based control of RL. Our approach augments Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) with a directional action-consistency regularization that softly aligns the RL agent's actions with VLA guidance during early training, without enforcing strict imitation, requiring demonstrations, or relying on continuous teacher queries. VLA guidance is applied sparsely and annealed over time, allowing the agent to adapt online and ultimately surpass the guiding policy. We evaluate VLAJS on six challenging manipulation tasks: lifting, pick-and-place, peg reorientation, peg insertion, poking, and pushing in simulation, and validate a subset on a real Franka Panda robot. However, their inference requires intensive processing of numerous visual tokens and incurs substantial computational overhead. To address these limitations, we introduce TOP-RL, a Task-Optimized Progressive token pruning framework based on Reinforcement Learning. TOP-RL formulates visual token pruning as a multi-stage Markov Decision Process (MDP). It employs an agent trained with dense and fine-grained reward signals to progressively generate differentiable binary masks. This enables TOP-RL to adaptively select crucial visual tokens tailored to each task, effectively balancing accuracy and computational efficiency. Extensive experiments on leading multimodal datasets and advanced LVLMs validate that TOP-RL effectively learns task-optimized pruning policies, significantly boosting inference efficiency while preserving robust performance. For instance, LLaVA-NeXT equipped with TOP-RL achieves a 1.9x speedup in inference time and a 9.3x reduction in FLOPs, with 96% performance preserved.

In addition to the adaptive inference of EE prediction for downstream tasks, self-distillation (SD) can encourage EE classifiers to mimic the behavior of the final classifier to enhance their representation capacity. However, the gradients from different tasks of EE classifiers will conflict and cancel one another out. The parameters of the backbone and some EE classifiers will be implicitly prevented from updating. Moreover, if the semantic gap between the final classifier and EE classifiers is significant, the EE classifier's performance will decrease. That is, the final classifier would not be the best choice to enhance the performance of the EE classifier. This study proposed an early exiting strategy with adaptive ensemble self-distillation and consistent gradients for the inference acceleration of PLMs. To mitigate gradient conflicts, we orthogonally projected the distillation loss's backpropagated gradient onto the classification loss's normal plane. Instead of directly using the final classifier as a single-teacher for self-distillation, we dynamically assemble different adaptive teachers for different EE classifiers according to the learning abilities of the EE classifiers. The accumulative decision was drawn for adaptive inference to make accurate and reliable predictions. Experimental results show that the proposed model outperforms existing models with the same speed-up ratio and effectively balances model performance and inference time.

We then introduce SkylineBuilder, an approach for dynamically deciding on which passage to allocate computation at each step, based on a resource allocation policy trained via reinforcement learning. Our results on SQuAD-Open show that adaptive computation with global prioritisation improves over several strong static and adaptive methods, leading to a 4.3x reduction in computation while retaining 95% performance of the full model.

AdaPonderLM uses iteration-specific MLP gates with a monotonic halting mask to decide when each token stops recurring, and introduces a KV reuse mechanism that reuses cached key/value states for halted tokens, ensuring train--test consistency and practical acceleration. Across Pythia backbones from 70M to 410M (pretraining) and up to 2.8B (continued pretraining), AdaPonderLM reduces inference compute at about 10% while maintaining comparable language modeling perplexity and competitive downstream accuracy. Our analysis shows the learned gates allocate more computation to high-NLL (hard) tokens, exhibiting adaptive computation time behavior in a fully self-supervised setting.    This work revisits the dominant supervised fine-tuning (SFT) then reinforcement learning (RL) paradigm for training Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs), and reveals a key finding: SFT can significantly undermine subsequent RL by inducing ``pseudo reasoning paths'' imitated from expert models. While these paths may resemble the native reasoning paths of RL models, they often involve prolonged, hesitant, less informative steps, and incorrect reasoning. To systematically study this effect, we introduce VLAA-Thinking, a new multimodal dataset designed to support reasoning in LVLMs. Constructed via a six-step pipeline involving captioning, reasoning distillation, answer rewrite and verification, VLAA-Thinking comprises high-quality, step-by-step visual reasoning traces for SFT, along with a more challenging RL split from the same data source. Using this dataset, we conduct extensive experiments comparing SFT, RL and their combinations. Results show that while SFT helps models learn reasoning formats, it often locks aligned models into imitative, rigid reasoning modes that impede further learning. In contrast, building on the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a novel mixed reward module integrating both perception and cognition signals, our RL approach fosters more genuine, adaptive reasoning behavior. Notably, our model VLAA-Thinker, based on Qwen2.5VL 3B, achieves top-1 performance on Open LMM Reasoning Leaderboard (https://huggingface.co/spaces/opencompass/Open_LMM_Reasoning_Leaderboard) among 4B scale LVLMs, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art by 1.8%. While prior surveys focus on efficiency by examining how to shorten reasoning chains or reduce computation, this view overlooks a fundamental challenge: current LLMs apply uniform reasoning strategies regardless of task complexity, generating long traces for trivial problems while failing to extend reasoning for difficult tasks. This survey reframes reasoning through the lens of {adaptivity}: the capability to allocate reasoning effort based on input characteristics such as difficulty and uncertainty. We make three contributions. First, we formalize deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning within the LLM context, connecting these classical cognitive paradigms with their algorithmic realizations. Second, we formalize adaptive reasoning as a control-augmented policy optimization problem balancing task performance with computational cost, distinguishing learned policies from inference-time control mechanisms. Third, we propose a systematic taxonomy organizing existing methods into training-based approaches that internalize adaptivity through reinforcement learning, supervised fine-tuning, and learned controllers, and training-free approaches that achieve adaptivity through prompt conditioning, feedback-driven halting, and modular composition. This framework clarifies how different mechanisms realize adaptive reasoning in practice and enables systematic comparison across diverse strategies. We conclude by identifying open challenges in self-evaluation, meta-reasoning, and human-aligned reasoning control.

1. You Need Multiple Exiting: Dynamic Early Exiting for Accelerating Unified Vision Language Model (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2022, cites: 53)
   Large-scale Transformer models bring significant improvements for various downstream vision language tasks with a unified architecture. The performance improvements come with increasing model size, resulting in slow inference speed and increased cost for severing. While some certain predictions benefit from the full computation of the large-scale model, not all of inputs need the same amount of computation to conduct, potentially leading to computation resource waste. To handle this challenge, early exiting is proposed to adaptively allocate computational power in term of input complexity to improve inference efficiency. The existing early exiting strategies usually adopt output confidence based on intermediate layers as a proxy of input complexity to incur the decision of skipping following layers. However, such strategies cannot be applied to encoder in the widely-used unified architecture with both encoder and decoder due to difficulty of output confidence estimation in the encoder layers. It is suboptimal in term of saving computation power to ignore the early exiting in encoder component. To address this issue, we propose a novel early exiting strategy for unified vision language models, which allows to dynamically skip the layers in encoder and decoder simultaneously in term of input layer-wise similarities with multiple times of early exiting, namely MuE. By decomposing the image and text modalities in the encoder, MuE is flexible and can skip different layers in term of modalities, advancing the inference efficiency while minimizing performance drop. Experiments on the SNLI-VE and MS COCO datasets show that the proposed approach MuE can reduce expected inference time by up to 50% and 40% while maintaining 99% and 96% performance respectively.

We build a multi-exit encoder-decoder transformer model which is trained with deep supervision so that each of its decoder layers is capable of generating plausible predictions. In addition, we leverage simple yet practical techniques, including shared generation head and adaptation modules, to keep accuracy when exiting at shallow decoder layers. Based on the multi-exit model, we perform step-level dynamic early exit during inference, where the model may decide to use fewer decoder layers based on its confidence of the current layer at each individual decoding step. Considering different number of decoder layers may be used at different decoding steps, we compute deeper-layer decoder features of previous decoding steps just-in-time, which ensures the features from different decoding steps are semantically aligned. We evaluate our approach with two state-of-the-art encoder-decoder transformer models on various VL tasks. We show our approach can reduce overall inference latency by 30%-60% with comparable or even higher accuracy compared to baselines.

By reconciling interpretability with computational efficiency, LO-VLM establishes a paradigm for efficient AI models in OCT interpretation. Eye diseases such as diabetic retinopathy and macular degeneration can cause vision loss if not detected early. Doctors use a type of imaging called optical coherence tomography (OCT) to examine the layers of the retina, but interpreting the images obtained can be time-consuming and requires specialist training. In this study, we developed LO-VLM, an artificial intelligence system that learns from both images and descriptions written by experts to enable analysis of OCT scans.
